summary: I'm originally from the San Francisco Bay Area in California. I was born in San Francisco and spent most of my formative years in San Mateo County, particularly in Foster City. Growing up there, I was exposed to the environmental issues of California, like water conservation and wildfires, which subtly influenced my later interest in environmental journalism.

California is where I grew up, and it shaped a lot of my early understanding of environmental challenges. I remember as a kid being told to conserve water during droughts and hearing about the wildfires on the news. The state's political ambition to tackle climate issues is quite different from places like Kentucky, where I work now, and that contrast has been eye-opening for me.

I attended the University of Missouri for journalism because I wanted to turn my love of writing into a career. Their program is very hands-on, and I got to write for the Columbia Missourian, covering various beats like public health, politics, business, and environment. It was during this time that I discovered my passion for environmental reporting.

Report for America is a program I joined after college that places emerging journalists in newsrooms around the country. I was placed in Louisville, Kentucky, with the Courier Journal through this program starting in June 2022. It's similar to Teach for America and AmeriCorps, and I renewed my position for a second year with an optional third year coming up.

Teach for America is a program similar to Report for America, which I joined after college. While I didn't participate in Teach for America myself, I understand that both programs focus on placing people in important roles for a couple of years, and Report for America follows a similar model with annual renewals and a typical two-year commitment.

AmeriCorps is another national service program like Report for America and Teach for America. I see Report for America as being in the same family of programs that place people in service roles for a limited time, often around two years. Although I didn't serve in AmeriCorps, I recognize the shared goal of community and public service among these organizations.

I have been living and working in Louisville, Kentucky since June 2022, where I cover the environment for the Courier Journal. Louisville is quite different from California, especially in terms of environmental issues like water abundance rather than scarcity. Covering the environment here has been a broad and engaging beat, including topics like energy, climate, natural disasters, and urban planning.

The Courier Journal in Louisville is where I work as an environmental reporter through Report for America. Since June 2022, I've covered a wide range of topics including energy, climate, natural disasters, and infrastructure. The paper has given me the opportunity to be part of the Mississippi River Basin Ag and Water Desk, collaborating with other environmental reporters in the region.
cues: Mississippi River Basin Ag and Water Desk

summary: As part of my role at the Courier Journal, I contribute to the Mississippi River Basin Ag and Water Desk, which is a collaboration of environmental reporters across the region. This basin includes the Ohio River Basin, and we work together to cover agriculture, water, and climate issues. Our stories are shared widely for republication throughout the region, which helps raise awareness about these interconnected environmental challenges.

The Ohio River Basin is part of the larger Mississippi River Basin that I cover through the Ag and Water Desk collaboration. This area is central to many environmental stories about water, agriculture, and climate that I report on for the Courier Journal. Working with other reporters in this basin allows us to share insights and stories that affect a broad region.

I was born in San Francisco, which is where my family on my mom's side mostly lives. We visit the city every year, and it remains an important place to me. Growing up near San Francisco exposed me to California's unique environmental challenges and culture, which shaped my early awareness of issues like water conservation and wildfires.

I spent much of my childhood in San Mateo County, particularly in Foster City. This area is where I grew up and attended San Mateo High School. Living there gave me a firsthand look at California's environmental landscape and community, which influenced my later interest in environmental topics.

Foster City is where I grew up within San Mateo County. It was my home during my formative years before I left for college. The community and environment there were part of my early life experiences that contributed to my eventual passion for environmental journalism.

I attended San Mateo High School in California, where I completed my secondary education. This school was part of my upbringing in the Bay Area before I moved to Missouri for college. My experiences there were foundational, though I didn't yet know I would pursue journalism or environmental reporting.

My dad lives in Marin County now, and I have family there as well, including an aunt. Marin County is a beautiful area near San Francisco that I visit occasionally. It represents another part of my California roots and family connections outside of the Bay Area.

Growing up in California, I remember hearing about the wildfires on the news, which were a significant environmental concern even when I was a kid. These wildfires were part of the broader environmental challenges the state faces, alongside drought and water conservation. They contributed to my early awareness of climate and environmental issues, even if I wasn't fully focused on them at the time.

While at the University of Missouri, I wrote for the Columbia Missourian, the university-run newspaper. This experience was crucial in helping me try out different beats like public health, politics, business, and environment. It was during this time that I realized I was most passionate about environmental reporting.

The New York Times is an example of a large newsroom that can afford to have multiple environmental reporters. I mentioned them when discussing the challenge of covering climate and environment beats alone in smaller newsrooms like mine. They represent a model where climate coverage can be more specialized and extensive.

SUNY Stony Brook was not directly mentioned in my story, so I don't have a personal connection or experience with that institution. My education and journalism training were centered at the University of Missouri.

entity: Alan Alda Center for Communicating Science
summary: The Alan Alda Center for Communicating Science wasn't part of my experience or discussion in this interview. My focus has been on journalism training at the University of Missouri and environmental reporting through Report for America.
